revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:和魂漢才]]


{{Nihongo|'''Japanese spirit, Chinese learning''|和魂漢才|Wakon Kansai}} is a term in Japanese intellectual history that represents the paired concepts of the native Japanese spirit "Yamatodamashii" ({{ill|Yamato-damashii|en|Yamato-damashii|qid=Q1758085|lt=Yamato spirit|1=Yamato-damashii}}){{Efn|Yamato damashii 大和魂 contains the kanji for Wakon 和魂. An abbreviated form is used in the 4 character idiom.}} and Chinese-derived learning "Karazae" ({{ill|Chinese talent|ja|漢才|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chinese talent|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}). It also signifies the integration of these two elements.<ref name="jiten">"{{ill|Wakon Kansai|ja|和魂漢才|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakon Kansai|1=Japanese spirit, Chinese learning|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11418249}}" ({{Harvnb|Iwanami Kokugo|2011-11|p=1615}})</ref>.

[[File:Ôsaka-ten'man-gû Shintô Shrine - Stone monument of Wakon-kansai-hi.jpg|thumb|170px|Monument of Wakon Kansai (Osaka Tenmangū, Osaka)]]
The concept was established in the mid-{{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|qid=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}, when the aristocratic ruling class placed the foundation of scholarship in Chinese texts, known as "Karazae." In contrast, practical knowledge, including judgment and conduct in daily life, was referred to as "Yamatodamashii." This mirrored the cultural pairing of {{ill|Shi (poetry)|en|Shi (poetry)|qid=Q1759242|lt=Chinese poetry|1=Shi (poetry)}} and {{ill|Waka (poetry)|en|Waka (poetry)|qid=Q737148|lt=Waka (poetry)|1=Waka (poetry)}}, as well as {{ill|Tang painting|ja|唐絵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tang painting|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Yamato-e|en|Yamato-e|qid=Q597365|lt=Yamato-e|1=Yamato-e}}.

The term "Wakon Kansai" appeared in the medieval pseudo-classic ''{{ill|Kanke Ika|ja|菅家遺誡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanke Ika|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'', which was composed in the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|qid=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period|1=Muromachi period}}. It was influenced by {{ill|Shinkoku thought|ja|神国思想|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinkoku thought|1=shinkoku thought|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q60985150}}, a nationalist ideology emerging after the Mongol invasions of Japan ({{ill|Mongol invasions of Japan|en|Mongol invasions of Japan|qid=Q208156|lt=Genkō War|1=Mongol invasions of Japan}}), and emphasized spiritual aspects over scholarly learning.

During the {{ill|Bakumatsu|en|Bakumatsu|qid=Q804558|lt=Bakumatsu|1=Bakumatsu}}, influenced by {{ill|Hirata school Kokugaku|ja|平田派国学|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hirata school Kokugaku|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the term took on a more {{ill|Japanese nationalism|en|Japanese nationalism|qid=Q1331440|lt=Nationalist ideology|1=Japanese nationalism}} and political connotation, particularly in support of {{ill|Sonno Joi|ja|尊皇攘夷|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sonno Joi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 尊王攘夷|1=Sonnō jōi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q1065356}} (Revere the Emperor, Expel the Barbarians). After Japan's victory in the {{ill|First Sino-Japanese War|en|First Sino-Japanese War|qid=Q178687|lt=First Sino-Japanese War|1=First Sino-Japanese War}}, the concept of "Kansai" (Chinese talent) was gradually discarded, leaving only "Wakon" (Yamato spirit). This evolved into the {{nihongo|{{ill|Yamato-damashii|en|qid=Q1758085|1=Yamato-damashii}}|大和魂||"Yamato/Japanese spirit"}}{{Efn|Yamato damashii 大和魂 contains the kanji for Wakon 和魂. An abbreviated form is used in the 4 character idiom.}} that underpinned {{ill|Militarism|en|Militarism|qid=Q177266|lt=militarism|1=Militarism}} and {{ill|Statism|en|Statism|qid=Q322294|lt=statism|1=Statism}} until Japan's defeat in {{ill|World War II|en|World War II|qid=Q362|lt=World War II|1=World War II}}.

{{Reflist}}

== Bibliography ==
*{{Citation|和書|editor1={{ill|Nishio Minoru|ja|西尾実|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishio Minoru|1=Minoru Nishio|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11627731}}|editor2={{ill|Iwabuchi Etsutarō|ja|岩淵悦太郎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwabuchi Etsutarō|1=Etsutarō Iwabuchi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11475351}}|editor3={{ill|Mizutani Shizuo|ja|水谷静夫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mizutani Shizuo|1=Shizuo Mizutani|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11549223}}|date=2011-11|title={{ill|Iwanami Kokugo Jiten|ja|岩波国語辞典|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwanami Kokugo Jiten|1=Iwanami Kokugo Jiten|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q28689262}}|edition=7th|publisher={{ill|Iwanami Shoten|en|Iwanami Shoten|qid=Q1331427|lt=Iwanami Shoten|1=Iwanami Shoten}}|isbn= 978-4000800471|ref={{Harvid|Iwanami Kokugo|2011-11}}}}
